segments.caption: The video opens with sweeping aerial views of major urban landscapes, beginning with San Francisco’s skyline under a hazy sky, featuring the Bay Bridge and a large cargo ship on the water. The scene transitions to New York City, showcasing the Brooklyn Bridge and Manhattan’s dense skyline under clear blue skies. This is followed by an aerial shot of a vast, organized agricultural field with rows of crops, then a desert landscape with a winding river and sparse vegetation. The narrative shifts to indoor settings, featuring a man in a light purple shirt and glasses speaking in an office with a framed certificate and books in the background. The video then cuts to a bustling city street with a time-lapse of pedestrians crossing a zebra crossing, followed by a crowded interior of Grand Central Terminal with people moving rapidly. Another man, wearing glasses and a dark blazer, is shown speaking outdoors. The scene changes to a lively outdoor market in a town with colorful stalls and people browsing. A woman in a gray top and black pants is seen bending over to fill a blue container from a public water spigot. An aerial view of a large industrial building with a red roof appears next, followed by a close-up of a man in a dark jacket and cap walking through a grassy field. The video then shows a factory interior with rows of green bottles on a conveyor belt, followed by a close-up of a man in a dark suit and tie speaking indoors. The final scenes include a wide shot of a vast field of white crops under a cloudy sky, and a man in a checkered shirt sitting at a desk in a modern office, speaking with his hands clasped.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Man in Checkered Shirt, seated in a modern office, glasses perched on his head, speaking thoughtfully. The scene quickly transitions to an outdoor shot of the Facebook headquarters sign at 1 Hacker Way, surrounded by trees under a clear sky. This is followed by a dramatic nighttime shot of a fountain with water jets illuminated against a dark building. The focus then shifts to the Man in Light Purple Shirt, seated in a well-lit office with large windows, gesturing as he speaks, with a framed certificate and books visible behind him. The video then cuts to a series of abstract and symbolic visuals: a water droplet creating ripples, a bottle pouring water into a glass, a close-up of a water droplet on a leaf, a swirling hurricane from space, a dry corn cob, and a hand sprinkling soil. These images culminate in a title card reading 'DocFilm DW THIRST When the Wells Run Dry' and 'The Last Resources A Film by Daniel Harrich' over a rippling water background. The final scenes show an aerial view of Potsdam, highlighting its historic buildings and greenery, followed by a close-up of a brick building with a plaque and a blue dome, suggesting a scientific or academic institution.

segments.caption: The video opens with a man in a dark suit and white shirt, identified as Prof. Anders Levermann from the Potsdam Institute for Climate Impact Research, seated on a high stool in a grassy field in front of a large, historic brick building with domes and arched windows. He gestures expressively as he speaks, with the camera alternating between wide shots of him in the field and close-ups of his face. The scene transitions to aerial and industrial footage: a dam releasing water, a factory bottling water, and a smokestack emitting plumes of smoke. The video then cuts to another man, Fred Hattermann, also from the Potsdam Institute, seated on a stool in a grassy area with scientific monitoring equipment in the background. He speaks while gesturing, with the camera shifting between medium and close-up shots. The segment continues with aerial views of industrial complexes and a car manufacturing plant, showing robotic arms welding car bodies. The sequence ends with a close-up of a robotic arm welding a white car frame, emphasizing industrial automation and environmental impact.

The camera then focuses on Stefan Kunerth, a man with short gray hair and glasses, wearing a blue button-up shirt and dark pants, seated on a stool in the factory. He speaks directly to the camera, with industrial machinery and a Krones machine visible in the background. The segment ends with a wide aerial shot of a rural landscape under a hazy sky, suggesting a shift to a broader environmental context.

segments.caption: The video opens with a construction site scene, showing a large yellow drilling machine behind a metal fence, with a worker in blue overalls and a white cap operating equipment and handling hoses, suggesting groundwater extraction or drilling work. The scene transitions to an indoor office setting where a man in a dark blazer and glasses, identified as Steffen Gärtner from the Town of Gellersen, speaks animatedly at a table, gesturing with his hands while seated in a formal meeting room with framed portraits on the wall. The narrative then shifts to a public protest, where a crowd gathers under umbrellas in a town square, holding signs with messages like 'WER UNSER WASSER NICHT SCHÜTZT, WIRD NICHT GEWÄHLT!' (If you don't protect our water, you won't get elected!) and 'Das Wasser gehört unseren Kindern' (The water belongs to our children). A woman sits on the ground with a sign, and a man in a blue vest speaks into a microphone from a podium, addressing the crowd. The atmosphere is one of civic concern over water protection, with the protest taking place in front of historic buildings under daylight, emphasizing community activism and environmental advocacy.

The video then shifts to a man in a light gray polo shirt and glasses, standing in front of a large screen displaying a satellite map of South Asia with color-coded 'Equivalent Height Anomaly' data, indicating groundwater changes over time. He gestures toward the map as the date progresses from 2005 to 2013, highlighting a growing red anomaly over northern India. The map then zooms out to show a broader view of the region, including parts of the Middle East and Central Asia, with the 'NASA GRACE Mission' text appearing, emphasizing the scientific data source. The scene cuts to a close-up of the same man, now in front of a different map showing groundwater changes in Germany, with a color scale in cm/year, as he points to specific regions, likely explaining regional water depletion trends. The overall narrative moves from personal and military stories to scientific visualization of global water scarcity, using satellite data to illustrate the severity and spread of groundwater loss over time.

segments.caption: The video begins with a man in a light gray polo shirt, identified as a scientist or researcher, standing in front of a large screen displaying a map titled 'Trends in Total Water Storage (cm/yr) from NASA/DSR GRACE and GRACE-FO (2002-2020)'. He gestures toward the map, which shows water storage anomalies across Europe, with red and orange areas indicating depletion. The scene transitions to a close-up of a line graph labeled 'JPL Mascons, Mascon #398, land', showing water equivalent height fluctuations from 2004 to 2020, with a downward trend indicated by a red dashed line. The video then cuts to an aerial view of a town, likely Lüneburg, with red-roofed buildings and a prominent church spire, followed by a shot of a large industrial building with the 'KRONES' logo, suggesting a bottling facility. The next scene shows a protest in a town square, with demonstrators holding signs such as 'WIR BRAUCHEN WASSER' and 'WIR BRAUCHEN EINE ZUKUNFT', and a large banner reading 'WEDER WASSER NOCH FÖRDERUNG'. The camera then focuses on a man in a dark suit and tie, identified as Jens Böther from Lüneburg, seated at a white table in a modern office with abstract art on the wall. He speaks directly to the camera, gesturing with his hands, discussing water management or policy issues. The setting remains consistent throughout his interview, with natural light and a professional atmosphere.

segments.caption: The video opens with a protest scene in a town square under overcast skies, where demonstrators, many wearing masks and holding umbrellas, carry signs with messages like 'WASSER IST LEBEN' (Water is Life) and 'Unser Wasser in Flaschen füllt nur Coca Colas Taschen!' (Our water in bottles fills only Coca-Cola's pockets!), indicating opposition to water extraction by Coca-Cola. The crowd is animated, clapping and chanting, with one protester holding a sign reading 'WIR SIND HIER, WIR SIND LAUT, WEIL VIO KLAUT' (We are here, we are loud, because VIO steals our water). The scene transitions to an aerial view of a large industrial complex with a prominent Coca-Cola European Partners building, followed by a close-up of a document dated January 18, 2022, announcing Coca-Cola's decision to halt plans for a third well in Lüneburg due to declining mineral water demand. The video then shifts to a wooded area where a blue van is parked near a concrete well cap, and a person in a blue jacket walks toward it. Inside a bright office, a woman with short gray hair, Dr. Marianne Temmesfeld, sits at a desk with a laptop displaying the Coca-Cola website, speaking to a man in a vest who leans on the desk. The final shot shows the exterior of a modern building with red Coca-Cola flags, including a rainbow Pride flag, fluttering in the wind, symbolizing corporate branding and public presence.

segments.caption: The video opens with a scene at a construction site where a man in a dark suit and scarf (Man in Dark Suit and Scarf) and a man in a blue blazer (Man in Blue Blazer) are being interviewed by reporters wearing hard hats and high-visibility vests. The setting is outdoors, with a large concrete structure in the background, and microphones are pointed at the two men as they speak. The camera then cuts to a wide aerial shot of a vast, receding reservoir, revealing exposed dry land and numerous boats stranded on the shore, indicating severe water depletion. The scene transitions to a man in a light green polo shirt and jeans (Jay Famiglietti) walking along a sandy shore toward the water, then sitting on a rock under a tree, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. The background features the same reservoir with boats and distant mountains. The video concludes with aerial views of the reservoir’s expansive, dry shoreline, followed by credits rolling over a shot of a smokestack emitting black smoke, with text identifying the film’s crew and location: 'Shot in Mexico' and 'A Film by DANIEL HARRICH'.

segments.caption: The video opens with a wide, static shot of a vast, arid, and barren landscape under a clear blue sky, suggesting a desert or drought-affected region. Sparse, dry shrubs dot the pale, cracked earth, and in the distance, faint industrial structures and power lines are visible, hinting at human presence. As the camera slowly pans right, the terrain remains desolate, with tire tracks cutting through the dry ground, emphasizing human activity in an otherwise lifeless environment. The scene gradually transitions to reveal a body of water in the distance, possibly a receding lake or reservoir, with the horizon line becoming more defined. The camera continues its slow pan, and the water becomes more prominent, appearing shallow and still. As the pan concludes, a large, rusted red sign with bold white letters spelling 'S.O.S.' emerges from the shallow water, partially submerged, standing as a stark symbol of distress and environmental crisis. The sign is anchored by metal supports, and its placement in the water underscores the severity of water loss. Throughout the sequence, white text overlays appear on the right side of the screen, crediting the production team: camera operators Walter Harrich and Ray Marmolejo, video editors Michael Fungone and Constantin Dauch, shoot in Mexico by Wolf-Dieter Vogel, production manager Thomas Lorenz, commissioning editor Thomas Reutter, producer Hans-Michael Kassel, executive producer Danuta Harrich-Zandberg, and production by DIWAFILM GMBH for SWR, with an English version by Deutsche Welle in 2022. The overall atmosphere is one of desolation, urgency, and environmental warning, with the 'S.O.S.' sign serving as the focal point of human desperation in the face of ecological collapse.
